seoTitle: How to Download an Excel Worksheet to Your Computer
metaDescription: Save an Excel worksheet locally from Microsoft 365 Web, Google Sheets, or an email attachment without breaking formulas or losing your formatting.
intro: Moving a live cloud worksheet back to your local drive shouldn't scramble your formulas or formatting. This guide covers downloading from Excel for the Web, converting a Google Sheets file into native .xlsx format, and pulling a worksheet straight out of an email attachment.

steps.heading: Open File in Excel for the Web
steps.body: In your browser-based workbook, click the File tab in the top left corner.

steps.heading: Go to Save As
steps.body: Navigate down the menu and click Save As.

steps.heading: Download a copy
steps.body: Click Download a Copy from the fly-out settings drawer.

steps.heading: Confirm the download
steps.body: Review the confirmation prompt and click Download to save the file locally.

steps.heading: Convert from Google Sheets if needed
steps.body: In Google Sheets, go to File, hover over Download, and select Microsoft Excel (.xlsx) to convert and save it.

steps.heading: Save an email attachment
steps.body: Hover over the attached workbook in your email, click the Download arrow, choose a destination folder, and save.

faq.question: Will my formulas still work after downloading?
faq.answer: Yes, the export process is designed to preserve formulas, number formats, and grid metadata when converting to a local .xlsx file.

faq.question: Can I download just one worksheet instead of the whole workbook?
faq.answer: The standard download options typically export the full workbook, so you may need to copy a specific sheet into a new file if you only want one tab.

faq.question: Is downloading from Google Sheets the same process as Excel for the Web?
faq.answer: The steps differ slightly, but both convert the cloud file into a native .xlsx format you can save locally.

faq.question: What should I check after downloading a file from email?
faq.answer: Double-click the saved file to confirm your formulas, fonts, and data ranges display correctly before relying on it.
